title: The Story of a Keystone
summary: 270+ species. 5000 years of human history. A disease that could end it all.

pages.title:
An Ecosystem
Lives Here.
pages.body: Over 270 species of birds, mammals, and insects depend on the Coast Live Oak for food and shelter. From acorn woodpeckers storing their harvest in its bark to the California oak moth whose caterpillars feed exclusively on its leaves.

pages.title:
5000 Years
On the Table.
pages.body: At least twelve distinct Native American cultures, including the Chumash, Ohlone, and Cahuilla, relied on Coast Live Oak acorns as a dietary staple. Ground into flour, leached of tannis, and cooked into bread or mush.

pages.title:
A Disease
Spreads Fast.
pages.body: Sudden Oak Death, caused by Phytophthora ramorum, has killed millions of tanoaks and Coast Live Oaks. It spreads through wind-blown rain, infected soil, and nursery plants — often outpacing detection and removal efforts.

pages.title:
Deep Roots,
Long Memory.
pages.body: A mature Coast Live Oak can live over 250 years and extend roots 30 meters in every direction. Its deep taproot accesses groundwater during California's long summer droughts, making it remarkably resilient.
